data.title: The Raging Conflict Between Israel And Iran, $100 Oil Might Be Possible
data.description:
The last couple of days now have been very interesting for market participants. Major market indices plummeted on June 13th, driven by worries about conflict between Israel and Iran. There has been a lot of rhetoric between the two nations over the last several decades, and there have been instances of fighting before. There are concerns, however, that this time could see the situation elevate to an entirely new level of catastrophe. Any sort of war, especially one that could see the US step in to defend an ally, poses downside risk for the market. Wars are costly, not only in terms of the lives lost, but also in terms of the wealth destroyed in them.

For most aspects of the economy, a widening of this conflict in the Middle East will prove to be a net negative. Off the top of my head, I can only think of two industries that might thrive should conflict get worse from here. The first and most obvious would be the defense industry. And the second would be the oil industry. In fact, even as major market indices declined, concerns about a potential supply disruption pushed crude prices higher. On June 13th alone, WTI crude prices jumped 7.6%, though at one point they were up more than 10%. This war premium, as you can appropriately call it, offers opportunities for oil investors. And it certainly, at least in my opinion, makes things a little less concerning for the industry for this year amidst trade war and broader economic concerns.

A look at the conflict

In what has been called Operation Rising Lion, the Israeli government made a bold decision to use over 200 of its aircraft and drones in order to conduct strikes on key military sites within Iran. This occurred in the early morning hours of June 13th and primarily centered around nuclear facilities. However, it also involved military, missile, and command sites within the nation. This included in the capital city of Tehran, but also involved several other cities as well. We don't know the death toll as of this writing. We may never know. But in all likelihood, hundreds have been killed from the attack.

The death toll included three incredibly powerful individuals in Iran. One of these was Hossein Salami, the head of Iran’s Revolutionary Guards. Another major casualty was Mohammad Bagheri, who served as the chief of staff of the country's armed forces. And then, there was Ali Shamkhani, who was a senior advisor to Ali Khamenei, the Supreme Leader of Iran who has controlled the country since assuming power in 1989.

This outbreak has become increasingly likely in recent years. Under the first Trump Administration, Donald Trump decided to put an end to a deal that had successfully prevented Iran from further developing its nuclear capabilities. Even though the country was in compliance with that deal, he felt as though their decision to continue developing other capabilities and to continue lobbing threats of violence against the US and Israel justified pulling out of the deal and slapping harsh sanctions on the country. Since then, there has been increased concern of the country further developing its nuclear capabilities and eventually taking significant military action against the US and/or Israel. The final straw was likely the fact that the IAEA, which is the nuclear watchdog of the United Nations, lambasted Iran recently for breaching its non-proliferation obligations after the country achieved a 60% enrichment rate for uranium earlier this year. This is the first time that the country has been deemed to be in breach of these obligations in almost 20 years.

Irrespective of your opinion of the situation, there is no denying that this attack by Israel served to escalate things further. I say this because additional attacks, including against other military bases and nuclear sites, as well as at the Mehrabad Airport outside of Tehran, spurred a retaliatory response by Iran against Israel. This included significant drone and ballistic missile launches, some of which have resulted in injuries and fatalities, and many of which were intercepted over not only Israel, but over Jordan, Syria, and Saudi Arabia. There has also been action by the Houthis in Yemen aimed toward Jerusalem.

These events, as well as any further escalation of them, will certainly prove to be bullish for the oil industry. Under the first Trump Administration, tough sanctions on Iran brought oil production down significantly. From 2017 through 2020, daily oil production from the country plummeted by 36.8% from 4.76 million barrels per day to 3.01 million barrels per day. Under the Biden Administration, however, oil production began rising again. This wasn't because sanctions on the country were lifted. Instead, it's because the country got creative when it came to shipping its oil and because of waivers granted to certain countries like China that enabled, on a temporary basis, increased buying of Iranian oil exports. The end result was a return to 4.68 million barrels per day last year.

Any sort of prolonged conflict with China risks causing oil production from that country to drop, perhaps significantly. Already, global oil markets had been in an interesting position. In the chart above, you can see daily production and consumption, on a global scale, for crude oil, as well as the excess supply during these years. Last year, for instance, the world produced around 0.06 million barrels per day more oil than it consumed. On its own, this is a small amount. But over the course of a year, it would total 21.9 million barrels of extra crude oil. For 2025, it was estimated that supply would be 0.82 million barrels per day above what demand called for. That would be an extra 299.3 million barrels of crude oil. And estimates provided by the EIA (Energy Information Administration) called for supply to outpace demand next year by 0.56 million barrels per day, amounting to an extra 204.4 million barrels of oil.

Oil prices are complex. Because the industry is denominated largely in U.S. dollars, changes in the strength of the dollar compared to other currencies can play a role in pricing. Worries about the economy can also affect pricing. But one interesting thing to keep in mind is that, typically, a balanced market for oil is one in which commercial OECD inventories range between 50 and 60 days' worth of supply. When you move outside this range, it's not uncommon to see big changes in pricing.

In the chart below, for instance, you can see how WTI crude pricing changed as commercial OECD inventories, as measured using days of supply, have changed as well. A jump in days of supply between 2013 and 2016 saw a corresponding decline in pricing. 2017 through 2019 saw a drop, with crude pricing rising. When there was a plunge in crude pricing in 2020 because of the COVID-19 pandemic, that was because of a surge in commercial OECD inventories caused by a major reduction in demand. And if we use estimates for this year and next year, we can see how a gradual increase in days of supply corresponds with a decline in pricing.

This disruption from Iran could easily take far more off the market than the gap that exists between global supply and global demand right now. But it's not just because of a potential reduction in crude oil being produced in Iran. It's also because of the possibility of disruption in the region more broadly. This has to do with the Persian Gulf and, within it, the Strait of Hormuz. Dubbed by Reuters in 2023 as ‘the world’s most important oil artery’, the area is responsible for around 20.5 million barrels of crude oil transport every day. This includes not only Iranian crude oil, but also oil from Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Iraq, and the UAE. Qatar is also affected because that country, which serves as the world's largest liquefied natural gas exporter, sends nearly all of its liquefied natural gas through that area.

All told, we are looking at around 20% of all oil used globally each day that flows through that region. And, knowing this, Iran has not been afraid to stir up trouble there. This has included action in most years, including in 2023, 2022, in 2021. Sometimes, that action is minor. But in some cases, like in January 2021, it can be more significant. In that month, the Iranian government seized a South Korean flagged tanker in Gulf waters and detained its crew. And in May 2019, four vessels, including two oil tankers owned by Saudi Arabia, were attacked just outside the Strait of Hormuz. Some of the countries involved can divert some of their oil away from that region. But a lot of it can't be diverted from it. Taking even 1 million barrels per day off of the global market through production decreases in Iran and disruptions at sea could in theory cause a massive jump in pricing. And if total war breaks out, as it is looking increasingly likely, it wouldn't be shocking to see crude oil jump to $100 per barrel or greater.

Takeaway

Right now, this is an interesting time for participants in the oil patch. In almost every respect, war is a bad thing. It costs money. It costs lives, it creates all sorts of issues. But for the oil industry, which has come under increasing pressure in recent months because of worries that the global economy will decline in light of the trade war situation that's going on, this could ultimately be a blessing. I, personally, don't like to bank on geopolitical conflicts. But for those who do like that sort of thing and who are generally bullish about oil, this might be a great time to consider jumping in to some of the players in the space, including oil and gas exploration companies like the ones that I cover at Crude Value Insights.

data.title: Apple: WWDC 2025 Was A Major Point Of Failure
data.description:
Was it really just as simple as "Steve Jobs"?

The recent Apple Worldwide Developer Conference, traditionally an event which marks bullish waypoints for the company, was an epic fail in a string of flops. The company continues to fall completely behind the curve on Artificial Intelligence and has failed to substantively innovate in ways that result in market-moving new products.

From 2002-2020, WWDC marked solid returns for Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) (TSX:AAPL:CA) far more often than not. But from 2021 to 2025, there has been a marked shift in that trend.

Year WWDC date Headline announcements (keynote highlights) 4-wk move* 12-wk move*
2002 May 6 Jaguar preview, iCal, Rendezvous (Bonjour), “funeral” for Mac OS 9 (Apple Special Events in 2002) ▼ 1.6% ▲ 11 %
2003 Jun 23 Power Mac G5, Xcode, Panther preview (Steve Jobs to Kick Off Apple's Worldwide Developers Conference 2003) ▲ 5.4% ▲ 21 %
2004 Jun 28 Tiger preview, new Cinema Displays (Steve Jobs to Kick Off Apple's Worldwide Developers Conference 2004 with Preview of Mac OS X "Tiger") ▲ 3.2% ▲ 14 %
2005 Jun 6 Intel transition revealed (List of Apple Inc. media events - Wikipedia) ▲ 1.0% ▲ 30 %
2006 Aug 7 Mac Pro, Leopard preview (List of Apple Inc. media events - Wikipedia) ▼ 6.3% ▲ 12 %
2007 Jun 11 iPhone OS 1 SDK details, Leopard refinements (List of Apple Inc. media events - Wikipedia) ▲ 4.9% ▲ 26 %
2008 Jun 9 iPhone 3G, App Store, iOS 2 (List of Apple Inc. media events - Wikipedia) ▼ 4.1% ▲ 8 %
2009 Jun 8 iPhone 3GS, Snow Leopard GM (List of Apple Inc. media events - Wikipedia) ▲ 0.7% ▲ 18 %
2010 Jun 7 iPhone 4 + FaceTime, iOS 4 (List of Apple Inc. media events - Wikipedia) ▲ 7.8% ▲ 23 %
2011 Jun 6 iOS 5, iCloud, OS X Lion (List of Apple Inc. media events - Wikipedia) ▼ 4.5% ▲ 9 %
2012 Jun 11 Retina MacBook Pro, iOS 6, Mountain Lion (List of Apple Inc. media events - Wikipedia) ▲ 3.9% ▲ 16 %
2013 Jun 10 iOS 7 redesign, OS X Mavericks, cylinder Mac Pro (List of Apple Inc. media events - Wikipedia) ▲ 0.4% ▲ 13 %
2014 Jun 2 Swift language debut, iOS 8, Yosemite (List of Apple Inc. media events - Wikipedia) ▲ 2.1% ▲ 12 %
2015 Jun 8 Apple Music launch, iOS 9, watchOS 2 (List of Apple Inc. media events - Wikipedia) ▼ 1.3% ▲ 6 %
2016 Jun 13 macOS Sierra, iOS 10, watchOS 3, tvOS 10 (List of Apple Inc. media events - Wikipedia) ▲ 2.9% ▲ 14 %
2017 Jun 5 iMac Pro, HomePod, iOS 11, High Sierra (List of Apple Inc. media events - Wikipedia) ▲ 7.1% ▲ 18 %
2018 Jun 4 iOS 12, macOS Mojave, watchOS 5 (List of Apple Inc. media events - Wikipedia) ▲ 1.6% ▲ 10 %
2019 Jun 3 Modular Mac Pro + Pro Display XDR, iPadOS, iOS 13 (List of Apple Inc. media events - Wikipedia) ▲ 5.5% ▲ 24 %
2020 Jun 22 Apple-Silicon transition (M1), Big Sur, iOS 14 (Apple Intelligence gets even more powerful with new capabilities across Apple devices) ▲ 15% ▲ 37 %
2021 Jun 7 iOS 15, macOS Monterey, watchOS 8 (no hardware) (List of Apple Inc. media events - Wikipedia) ▲ 2.2% ▲ 11 %
2022 Jun 6 M2 MacBook Air, iOS 16, macOS Ventura (Apple Recaps 18 Key Announcements From WWDC 2024 Keynote in Short Video) ▲ 0.9% ▲ 9 %
2023 Jun 5 Vision Pro reveal, 15″ MacBook Air, iOS 17 (Apple WWDC 2024 keynote in 18 minutes) ▼ 1.0% ▲ 6 %
2024 Jun 10 “Apple Intelligence,” iOS 18, macOS Sequoia, visionOS 2 prep (WWDC 2024: Top Keynote Highlights From Apple's WWDC) ▼ 4.3% ▲ 7 %
2025 Jun 9 Liquid Glass UI, iOS 26, macOS Tahoe 26, on-device AI model exposed to devs (Apple Intelligence gets even more powerful with new capabilities across Apple devices, WWDC 2025: all the news from Apple's annual developer conference) n/a n/a

On average, post-WWDC performance has slipped badly since 2020:

Metric 2002-2020 (avg %) 2021-2024 (avg %) Δ recent-vs-legacy
4-week move +2.3 % -0.6 % −2.9 %
12-week move +17.0 % +8.4 % −8.6 %

The median WWDC between 2002-2020 produced a modest but positive 4-week bump. But since 2021 that average has gone slightly negative, suggesting traders have begun fading the event in the short term. The stock is currently down about 2.5% post-WWDC 2025.

Over twelve weeks, legacy WWDCs were rewarded with rallies in the mid-teens to high-20s. Recent WWDCs have barely managed mid-single digit gains.

Relative Performance vs. Nasdaq 100

Bucket AAPL 4 wk QQQ 4 wk AAPL – QQQ AAPL 12 wk QQQ 12 wk AAPL – QQQ
2002-2020 +2.3% +-0.7% ▲ +3.0% +17.0% +1.9% ▲ +15.1%
2021-2024 -0.6% +0.0% ▼ -0.6% +8.4% +7.6% ▲ +0.8%

From 2002-2020, Apple routinely crushed the Nasdaq-100 around WWDC, beating the index by roughly +15 percentage-points over the 12-week window (and by about +3% over 4 weeks). In the most recent stretch (2021-2024) the “WWDC alpha” has almost vanished: Apple now lags the Nasdaq-100 by –0.6% on average after four weeks and edges it by only +0.8% after twelve weeks. In short, the stock’s post-WWDC edge over the tech benchmark that it is supposed to lead has been cut to one-tenth of what it used to be.

WWDC 2025

This year Apple led its announcements with "Liquid Glass" a whimsical UI software enhancement.

It's possible that the "upgrade" might make the user experience a bit more fun and engaging if it's well-implemented, but there are many initial reports that it degrades the device experience. In the best case, this is not going to sell more Apple devices versus competing hardware that is Artificial Intelligence integrated and capable.

The common thread in criticisms of the company is that it is leaning on software and not innovating on hardware. But the fact is that it when it has tried to break new ground on hardware, it has failed badly.

Hardware Failures

The Apple autonomous electric vehicle "Project Titan" (Apple Car) was abruptly cancelled after spending an estimated $10–15 billion and employing 2,000 engineers in February 2024.

Many on the team were reassigned to generative-AI work—an implicit admission that Apple no longer saw the car as a viable growth engine. If that reallocation of resources had resulted in an actual Apple Intelligence that was delivering competitive consumer features across its devices now, then we might hail the decision. But it has not. It resulted in a total failure to deliver anything except a weak attempt to rebrand the letters "AI" to reflect the company's ego ethos.

Then there's the Vision Pro WWDC announcement in 2023. I ridiculed that thing when it came out and saw instantly that it would bomb. Apple reportedly paused production in late-2024 amid excess inventory; sales sat at roughly 370k units after three quarters. Fewer than 500k units are expected to ship this year—about half Apple’s original goal.

Apple broke a 13-year streak by unveiling no new hardware category in 2024, doubling-down on incremental iPhone/iPad/Mac updates instead.

If Apple fails to unveil a hardware or platform shift of significant magnitude soon, its long-running secular trendline will finally break under the pressure.

Long-Term Trend Threatened

As you can clearly see in the chart above, the long-term weekly close trendline from 2009 as well as horizontal price support is at risk of being broken.

Let's zoom in:

A break of the long-term trend and horizontal price support comes at about 183, or about -8% from current levels. Price is not consolidating bearishly below its now-declining 50-week exponential moving average. Weekly RSI shows signs of entering a bearish range shift, failing to get back to its mid-range even as the rest of the tech space flirts with all-time highs.

AAPL has not kept pace with the rest of the tech space in the rally off the Trump Tariff Tantrum lows. It has rallied about half of the rest of the Nasdaq 100:

While QQQ has seen a bullish recross of the 50 day EMA above the 200 day EMA...

AAPL has seen its EMAs turn downward in a bearish configuration and shows signs of an accelerating angle of descent.

Since the 2021 price high, Apple has gained only 9% versus 13% for the equal-weighted QQEW and 33% for the cap-weighted QQQ:

Since the December 2022 low, AAPL has rallied about the same as the equal weighted index vs. about half of the capitalization weighted index:

Simply put, the leader is no longer leading. The first part of the fall of an old-line company that has seen its glory days is the surrender of the leadership position. By every measure, Apple has accomplished that.

The technical position of AAPL contrasts sharply with that of the SPX, NDX and other assets, as detailed in this article.

Financials Supportive--But For How Long?

While Apple’s balance sheet remains formidable (≈ $163 bn cash & marketable securities, net cash ≈ $59 bn) and buybacks continue to lower the share count, top‑line growth has stalled at a 2% CAGR since FY‑21 while R&D spend is rising at 12% CAGR.

Wall Street still demands ~5 % revenue CAGR and ~8 % EPS CAGR (FY‑24→FY‑26) in its consensus models, which leaves Apple pacing ~300 bps below topline expectations and relying on buy‑backs to bridge the EPS gap.

Valuation no longer bakes in growth optionality: at 27× NTM EPS, Apple trades at a ~15 % premium to its 10‑year median yet delivers sub‑single‑digit revenue growth—creating downside if no new hardware cycle emerges.

So financially, the company can weather a period of low growth, but not forever. And missing the boat entirely on the single greatest paradigm shift in history that is rolling at an exponentially accelerating pace puts real pressure on that weathering period.

What Time Is It?

Apple needs to check its Watch and figure out what time it is.

We are entering the Age of Intelligence. Going forward, everything about everything is delivering Intelligence to economically useful and productive endpoints. Everything about everything is in the process of being remade to do that. Everyone knows that the "next big thing" for the consumer market is the thing that connects people to Intelligence that they can use and that makes life better.

And until recently, everyone expected the company that would deliver that would be the company that has always delivered the next-paradigm way of interacting with information and data, that has the market-leading advantage, that has the capital resources and talent: Apple.

Only now it is the time when fewer and fewer and fewer people expect that from Apple, especially post-WWDC 2025, when the company presented absolutely nothing that reflects the urgency of the opportunity that if quickly turning itself into the next Intel.

That's what time it is, and Apple seems content to ride the banked good-will and cash stockpile of the past into the sunset of its years. The risk is that Apple is devolving into a has-been company while a whole new crop of leaders emerge in the tech space. Investors who are "Seeking Alpha" should exit AAPL and look for alpha elsewhere. Apple risks becoming at best an old-school underperformer in a new paradigm market.

Risks to This Analysis

Can Apple right the ship and bounce off that long term trendline? What would it take? Here are some factors that in some combination could stave off a secular decline in the stock:

  • Apple uses some of its cash hoard to buy some AI companies to get back in the game. Like the Lakers landing LeBron. Scooping some viable tech and the teams that come with them, the "acquihire" strategy, would be the fastest and perhaps the most effective way for Apple to remain relevant, at least perceptually, if not practically. Many in fact expect this to happen. And it sure could. And that could attract enough new buyers to AAPL to prevent a long-term breakdown.
  • Apple reveals AI tech that they have been secretly cooking in the background. Low probability, but not impossible. If they had a winning hand to show, WWDC 2025 would have been the time to put them on the table. But who knows, surprise announcements happen.
  • Apple announces a breakthrough in another area of tech that opens new market potential for the company.
  • Apple uses its cash hoard to buy back its own stock to prop up the price.

Oh yeah, lifelong Apple user here writing this on their Windows laptop. Not a "hater". Just really disappointed with the company at this juncture after WWDC 2025.

data.title: Ambac Financial Group: Conversations To Acquire Competitors, News About Oaktree Capital, And Cheap
data.description:
Ambac Financial Group, Inc. (NYSE:AMBC) could receive $420 million from Oaktree Capital soon. Moreover, the company reports ongoing conversation to buy one or more businesses. In my view, the new cash and M&A deals will most likely bring a number of new investors interested in the company. Besides, in the last quarterly report, the company noted ongoing investments in the insurance distribution business division to generate beneficial margins at scale. If we also add the company's ongoing stock repurchases and the fact that the total share count recently declined, I believe that we could see an increase in the stock price in the coming months. In 2014, the company traded at more than $30 per share, and in 2024, Ambac bought its own shares for $12 per share. Right now, we can buy at $6-$7 per share.

Ongoing Business Strategies, And Ambac Trades At 0.39x FWD Book Value Per Share

Ambac is an insurance holding company headquartered in New York City. The largest assets owned are Everspan, focused on Casualty Insurance business, and Cirrata, which is the company's insurance distribution division. Ambac reports a market capitalization not far from $315 million and a total headcount of 380 employees.

Ambac trades at 0.39x FWD book value per share after delivering lower than expected EPS GAAP figures in the last quarter. Peers trade at 1x FWD book value per share. In the last quarter, EPS GAAP figures stood at close to -$1.22 per share.

With the company trading at price levels never seen, it is worth knowing the plans of Ambac for the next years. Ambac signed agreements to sell certain parts of the business model, and has lowered its total amount of debt. However, management also came with a number of operating initiatives. First, the company expects to invest in niche business segments that offer margins at scale. In addition, Ambac expects to grow its specialty property and casualty insurance business division in the coming years. With a substantial amount of cash, a lot of expertise in the insurance industry, and lower debt than in the past, in my view, Ambac appears well positioned to make these investments. The following words have been taken from the company's most recent quarterly report.

Expanding our Insurance Distribution business based on deep domain knowledge in specialty and niche classes of risk which generate attractive margins at scale. Source: 10-Q

Growing our Specialty Property and Casualty Insurance business to generate underwriting profits from a diversified portfolio of commercial and personal liability risks accessed primarily through program administrators. Source: 10-Q

Repurchase Of Shares At $12, $13, And $8 Per Share

In 2024, the company reported a stock repurchase program, which, I believe, could accelerate the demand for the stock in the future. In my opinion, investors out there may buy shares because Ambac is buying. According to information given by Ambac, in the past, the company bought shares at $8, $13, and $12 per share.

Besides, it is worth noting that Ambac appears to have reduced its total share count, which may also have a positive effect on the company's implied stock valuation. In this regard, I would read the following words from the last quarterly report. Note that in the last quarter, the share count decreased as a result of the stock repurchases.

Ambac’s Board of Directors authorized a share repurchase program, under which Ambac may opportunistically repurchase up to $50,000 of the Company’s common shares at management’s discretion over the period ending on December 31, 2026. Source: 10-Q

Common shares outstanding decreased by 79,552 during the three months ended March 31, 2025, primarily due to share repurchases partially offset by shares issued in connection with employee stock compensation. Source: 10-Q

The Sale Of Shares To Oaktree Capital

Ambac reached an agreement to sell shares of AAC to Oaktree Capital for a total of $420 million in cash. Oaktree is an investment manager, reporting close to $192 billion in assets under management. Ambac reports a total market capitalization that appears lower than $400 million. Hence, in my view, if the deal closes successfully, Ambac will most likely see significant stock returns.

On June 4, 2024, AFG entered into a stock purchase agreement with American Acorn Corporation (the “Buyer”), a Delaware corporation owned by funds managed by Oaktree Capital Management, L.P., pursuant to which and subject to the conditions set forth therein, AFG will sell all of the issued and outstanding shares of common stock of AAC, a wholly-owned subsidiary of AFG, to Buyer for aggregate consideration of $420 in cash (the "Sale"). Source: 10-Q

With that being said about the agreement, the buyer is taking some time to obtain the regulatory approval. According to the last quarterly report, shareholders of Ambac will have to wait until July 3, 2025, to receive some information about the state of the deal. Given everything mentioned and the current valuation of the company, I do see significant upside potential in the stock price and limited downside risks.

The Buyer continues to pursue the final outstanding regulatory approval for the Sale, which would be received only after a hearing at or prior to which third parties would have an opportunity to object to the Sale. As a result, consistent with the terms of the purchase agreement, the term of the purchase agreement has been automatically extended from April 4, 2025, to July 3, 2025. Source: 10-Q

New Acquisitions Could Be Announced, And Could Enhance Demand For The Stock

With or without cash to be received from Oaktree Capital, the company appears to be checking new opportunities to buy other competitors out there in the insurance distribution industry. Ambac reports a large amount of accumulated goodwill, which means that the company has a lot of experience in M&A negotiation.

In my opinion, if Ambac buys at convenient prices, and investors appreciate the acquisitions, we could see demand for the stock. In the last quarterly report, Ambac provided the following information about ongoing discussions to acquire peers.

Ambac continuously evaluates, and is currently evaluating, opportunities to acquire businesses and assets for its Insurance Distribution business, and is currently in ongoing discussions to potentially acquire one or more businesses. These acquisitions may be material to our business, financial condition and operations and may involve raising capital to finance the acquisition(s). Source: 10-Q

My Valuation Figures: The Company Could Be Worth $18 Per Share

I made several conservative assumptions with regard to future free cash flow. I also used assumptions with regard to new companies being acquired, and investments in businesses that offer margins at scale.

I assumed that free cash flow will most likely range between $70 million in 2025 and $115 million in 2035. It is worth noting that the company reported more than $400 million in FCF in the past. However, in some years, the free cash flow was also negative.

If we also include a WACC of 10%, net debt of $115 million, 46 million shares, and 5x FCF, the stock price would not be far from $18 per share. Financial advisors using other valuation methods using trading multiples or the book value per share may get different results. However, I believe that we would most likely agree on one thing. Ambac appears quite undervalued at the current price of $6-$7 per share. The company is trading not far from its lowest price marks in the last ten years.

Risks

In my view, if the sale to Oaktree Capital does not take place, investors out there may sell their stakes. As a result, I think that we could see substantial declines in the stock price. It is worth noting that many investors out there may be buying shares because they expect new cash coming from Oaktree Capital. I would not advise to sell shares in any case. If the company closes the transaction, the stock price will most likely increase. On the contrary, if Ambac does not receive cash, in my view, the stock price may take some months to trend up again.

In May 2025, Ambac noted strong premium growth, and reported expectations of $80 million and $90 million adjusted EBITDA in 2028. The company intends to expand its MGA platform, which could accelerate the company's financials. In my view, if the company fails to deliver these numbers in the coming years, many investors may sell their shares. Ambac did deliver lower than expected EPS and cash flow figures in the past. It may happen again.

Ambac reports a considerable amount of debt. Hence, in my opinion, changes in the interest rates could have a large impact on future net income growth, or free cash flow growth. As soon as some analysts understand that Ambac may lower its results because of issues with debt holders, the demand for the stock could lower. Let's keep in mind that Ambac may need debt financing to launch marketing campaigns, pay employees, and design new products.

My Final Words: The Company Appears Quite Undervalued At 0.39x FWD book value

Oaktree Capital could pay $420 million to the company if regulators approve the deal. If Ambac does not receive regulator's approval, I would not really care because the company appears to be trading very undervalued. With cash in hand, in my view, proposed investments in the specialty property insurance business and the business that offers margins at scale could accelerate free cash flow growth. In addition, in the last quarterly report, Ambac noted ongoing negotiations to purchase other competitors. New M&A deals could bring a lot of attention from the investment community, and enhance the demand for the stock. Finally, if Ambac continues buying its own shares, and the share count declines at some point, the stock price will most likely trend higher. We are talking about a company that trades at 0.39x FWD book value per share.

data.title: The Best Offense Is A Good Defense: 5 Strong Buys To Combat Volatility
data.description:
I’m Steven Cress—and welcome to Seeking Alpha Quant. In this article, I’ll discuss my top five safe haven stocks to shield your portfolio against volatility in the face of geopolitical unrest. Each one exhibits strong fundamentals, excellent valuation frameworks, above average growth, and a resilient balance sheet to back up its Strong Buy rating.

U.S. Stocks Sink as Iran and Israel exchange Missiles

Stocks tumbled across Wall Street on Friday after Israel's military confirmed that Iran had fired ballistic missiles against Israel. The move followed an attack by Israel late on Thursday of Iranian nuclear sites and military targets, igniting fears of an all-out war in the Middle East.

The S&P 500 (SPY) fell 1.1%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) lost 1.3% and the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) shaved off more than 700 points, or 1.8%.

Market bulls, including Nvidia (NVDA) (-2.2%) that led the stock market’s comeback in April, shouldered losses at the end of the week, while energy and defense stocks like Exxon (XOM) (+2.16%) and Lockheed Martin (LMNT) (+2.66%) were boosted higher.

Meanwhile, commodities prices have only increased investor anxiety: WTI crude oil futures (CL1:COM) and Brent crude futures (CO1:COM) surged by 7.9% and +7.1%, respectively. Gold futures exploded as the heightened geopolitical turmoil pushed investors into safe haven assets, hitting an end-of-day record of $3,431.20 per troy ounce on Friday.

Seeking Safe Havens: Gold Hits Record End-of-Day High

The fast-escalating conflict has placed additional strain on global equities markets already under pressure from tariffs and rising inflation. As Seeking Alpha analyst Lawrence Fuller wrote on Friday:

Tariffs and trade policies are set to drive inflation higher, while front-loaded demand and weak tourism will further weigh on economic growth… Geopolitical tensions and rising oil prices threaten to push inflation up, delaying Fed rate cuts and raising the odds of a summer market correction.

A cooling labor market also comes as an ominous signal for the economy. Fed funds futures traders have upped their bets that the Federal Reserve will slash interest rate cuts this year after new data from the U.S. The Department of Labor showed that claims for insured employment for the week ending May 31 hit its highest level since November 2021.

All of these factors have come together to promote extreme fear in the market. The CBOE Volatility Index, otherwise known as Wall Street’s “fear gauge,” surged nearly 17% on Friday, to 21.08.

Wall Street’s ‘Fear Gauge’ Enters Anxiety Territory

In general a Vix level higher than 20 is reflective of investor anxiety, given that its 10-year closing average hovers around 18.5. In times like this, it has never been more critical to maintain a long-term perspective, "Keep Calm and Carry On," and avoid panic-selling. Likewise, allocating cash or rotating into safe-haven securities can also be prudent.

You may recall Donald Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariffs April 2, which led the Vix to spike above 50—one of the highest levels seen since the COVID-19 pandemic. The current reading of 20.08 hardly compares, serving as a reminder that this kind of market uncertainty is always temporary. Nevertheless, the volatility on Friday instilled enough fear into investors to begin rotating into safe-haven sectors.

Rising Middle East Unrest Triggers Safe-Haven Rush

Shifting Gears: Defensive Stocks Back in Play

Nonetheless, as fresh volatility grips global markets and the Iran-Israel conflict grows more severe, investors should remember that the best offense is a good defense.

We all know that uncertainty is a headwind to growth and normalized markets. Hence, as the conflict in the Middle East, tariff uncertainty, and inflation continue to loom over the market, we potentially could see downside to the market or the market getting stuck in a tight range.

Rather than allowing our fears to inform knee-jerk, emotional investment decisions, we can use it as a motivator to better hedge our portfolios against potential uncertainty. As I mentioned in my recent webinar, High-Conviction Stocks For Tariff Uncertainty, the market’s rotation into cash, consumer staples, REITs, healthcare and other defensive sectors led a lot of investors to diversify their portfolios for a volatile year.

Shield and Yield: A Barbell Approach to Defensive Investing

I often preach the idea of utilizing a barbell strategy in tumultuous markets. The idea is to combine fundamentally strong stocks with safer stocks that offer an element of non-cyclicality and/or dividends to generate steady income in volatile markets.

A barbell approach in investing can be advantageous because it combines two different types of securities, with safer, stable investments on one end and higher-risk, high-reward investments on the other. This strategy helps protect capital during market downturns by balancing risk while providing exposure to growth opportunities, allowing investors to navigate uncertainty and volatility more effectively.

So, instead of dumping your positions in fundamentally strong stocks that may have recently come under pressure, hold onto those investments while also targeting safe-haven stocks that can help balance out your portfolio.

Using Seeking Alpha’s Quant System, I hand-curated this safe-haven stock screener with the following criteria:

  • Strong Buy Quant Rating

  • Market cap greater than $500M

  • Hails from a defensive sector/industry (REITs, aerospace and defense, consumer staples, pharmaceuticals, telecommunications, utilities, energy, healthcare)

From that list, I identified five of my favorites from a diverse range of sectors and geographies to help investors de-risk while also capturing potential upside should the market rebound.

CTRE is a very fundamentally financially sound REIT that is operating in a massively growing area of the market - senior housing. It's clear that there are large numbers of seniors across America and the UK, and all of these citizens will need care and housing in the future. The company appears poised to be able to service these individuals and is making the right investments to address this growing population demand.

CTRE Dividend Scorecard (as of 6/13/25)

With a dividend yield of 5.5%, Imperial Brands is a safe haven choice for income-inclined investors. The London-listed maker of Winston cigarettes has gained more than 55% over the past year by prioritizing next-generation products ((NGPs)) such as vapes, e-cigarettes, and pouches. It also announced an additional £1.25B share buyback for FY25, a 13.6% increase from the prior fiscal year.

As SA analyst Philipp Brohl recently wrote:

The current share buyback program is the main driver for growth going forward. The higher the share price goes, the less EPS and dividend growth Imperial Brands will be able to achieve. While the current valuation of the shares are still attractive compared to other sectors, the air is getting thinner.

IMBBY Stock’s Quant Breakdown

IMBBY boasts a strong valuation, with an A- forward PEG ratio of 1.16, 8 58% discount, and an A+ forward GAAP P/E ratio of 9.89, a 48% discount.

With A’s across the board regarding its Dividend Yield Grade, IMBBY has proven its ability to reward shareholders. Impressive metrics include its four-year average dividend yield of 7.48%, a 186% difference from the sector, and a free cash flow yield of 11.4%, a 117% difference.
